title: JXL_PGO_MODULE_JS environment variable is imported directly as a dynamic module without any validation
description: The script reads the file path of the JS module to load entirely from the environment variable JXL_PGO_MODULE_JS (line 6) and then calls `import(pathToFileUrl(moduleJs))` (line 14) with no sanitization, path-traversal check, or allow-list. An attacker who can set environment variables (e.g. via CI misconfiguration or compromised upstream step) can supply an arbitrary absolute path and cause arbitrary JS to execute under the Node process. The custom `pathToFileUrl` at line 245 also accepts Windows-style backslash paths, broadening the attack surface on Windows hosts.

title: benchmarkModule imports an arbitrary JS file path supplied by the caller with no validation
description: The exported `benchmarkModule` function (line 56) calls `import(pathToFileURL(moduleJs).href)` where `moduleJs` is a caller-supplied string coming from CLI arguments (lines 239-244) without any sanitization or allowlisting. An attacker controlling the CLI arguments can supply a path to any `.js` file on disk to execute arbitrary code in the Node process. The CLI argument parser at line 228 treats any string after `--baseline-js`, `--candidate-js`, etc. as a valid path with no checks.

title: LIBJXL_REPO environment variable is passed unsanitized to git clone as the remote URL
description: The `config.libjxlRepo` value at line 24 comes directly from `process.env.LIBJXL_REPO` with no validation. It is used as the remote URL argument to `git clone` at line 586. An attacker who can set this env var in CI can supply a malicious git URL (e.g. a local path, an ssh:// URL that runs commands, or an http:// URL pointing to a malicious repo) to exfiltrate secrets or plant backdoored source code that gets compiled into the WASM artifact. The `LIBJXL_COMMIT` pin (line 592-594) provides a partial guard but only after checkout, and only for the top-level repo; submodule fallbacks do not pin their URLs in the same way.
code_excerpt:
const config = {
  buildId: "jxl-wasm-0.1.0",
  libjxlRepo: process.env.LIBJXL_REPO ?? "https://github.com/libjxl/libjxl.git",
  libjxlCommit: process.env.LIBJXL_COMMIT ?? "332feb17d17311c748445f7ee75c4fb55cc38530",

title: LIBJXL_REPO environment variable is passed unsanitized to git clone in build.mjs as well
description: Same pattern as security-3 but in the main build script. `config.libjxlRepo` (line 22) comes from `process.env.LIBJXL_REPO` and is forwarded verbatim to `git clone` in `clonePinnedSource` (lines 473-487). Additionally `DOCKER_BIN` and `EMSDK_IMAGE` env vars are reflected into docker command arguments (lines 302-343) without validation, widening the injection surface further. A CI environment where any of these env vars are attacker-controllable can result in malicious code being pulled and built into the shipped WASM.

title: expandGlob accepts glob patterns from the manifest JSON without preventing directory traversal above the manifest root
description: The `expandGlob(root, pattern)` function (line 330) normalizes the pattern but does not verify that the resolved directory path stays within `root`. A manifest file containing a pattern like `../../sensitive/*.ppm` or an absolute path would be resolved relative to the manifestDir without any containment check. This means a maliciously crafted `pgo-manifest.json` (e.g. injected via a pull-request or compromised corpus repository) can cause the build script to read files anywhere on the filesystem, leaking their contents into the corpus hash or processing them as image data.

title: PGO lock file is read and parsed without integrity verification, allowing a prior build artifact to supply attacker-controlled scenario data
description: In `runApplyStage` (line 128) the existing lock file is read via `readExistingLock()` (line 666) and its `scenarios` array is used directly without any cryptographic verification against a trusted source. An attacker who can write to the `dist/pgo-manifest.lock.json` file (e.g. via a compromised CI artifact upload step, or a race condition on a shared build host) can inject arbitrary scenario names and file paths that the subsequent training/apply steps will consume. The scenario files themselves would then be processed through the WASM encoder in the training run.

title: BoxDownscaleRgba8 does not validate that sw, sh, dw, dh are non-zero before integer division, risking divide-by-zero in the ceiling-division path
description: In `BoxDownscaleRgba8` (line 1093) the early return guards only `dw == 0 || dh == 0` (line 1095), but the general-case ceiling-division path at lines 1127-1145 computes `(dy * sh + dh - 1u) / dh` and `(dx * sw + dw - 1u) / dw`, which are safe. However the fast-path at line 1099 checks `sw % dw == 0 && sh % dh == 0`, which will also trap on dw=0 or dh=0 — but these are already guarded. More critically, there is no check that `sw > 0` and `sh > 0` before the ceiling path divisions at lines 1126-1130 compute `(dy * sh) / dh`; if `sh == 0` the fast-path is not taken and the scalar path proceeds with `y1 = (0 + dh - 1u) / dh = 0`, so the inner loops do not execute — no immediate crash but the output is silently zeroed. Additionally, a caller passing extremely large `sw` or `sh` values could cause `(dy * sh + dh - 1u)` to overflow `uint32_t` in the ceiling division.

title: runEmscripten uses shell:true on Windows for .bat files, enabling shell meta-character injection from attacker-controlled paths
description: The `runEmscripten` function (line 665) passes `shell: true` to `child_process.spawn` when the command ends in `.bat` on Win32 (lines 666-668). The command string at this point is derived from `EMSDK` env var (resolveEmscriptenBinary, line 680). If an attacker can set `EMSDK` to a path containing shell metacharacters (e.g. `C:\emsdk& calc` or a path with spaces and unquoted arguments), this enables command injection through the Windows shell. The `run` helper otherwise correctly uses `shell: false`.

title: runEmscripten in build-pgo.mjs also uses shell:true for .bat files, same injection risk as build.mjs
description: Identical pattern to security-8. The `runEmscripten` helper in build-pgo.mjs (line 753) sets `shell: true` for `.bat` commands on Windows, sourced from `EMSDK` env var. An attacker-controlled `EMSDK` can inject shell commands. This script additionally passes PGO profile paths (containing process.pid and Date.now()) as command arguments — not directly attacker-controlled, but the shell:true flag makes the entire args array vulnerable to shell expansion if any element contains metacharacters.

title: Custom pathToFileUrl does not validate the input path, allowing hand-crafted file:// URLs with directory traversal sequences
description: The local `pathToFileUrl` implementation (line 244) simply replaces backslashes and prepends `file:///` without normalizing `../` sequences or verifying the result stays within expected boundaries. If `JXL_PGO_MODULE_JS` contains a path with `..` components (e.g. `../../some/other/script.js`), the resulting `file:///` URL is valid and will be dynamically imported. This compounds security-1 but is independently reportable as a validation gap.

title: No schema validation on the PGO manifest JSON before its fields are used in build commands and file-system operations
description: The `normalizeManifest` function at line 260 coerces fields with `String()` and `Number()` but applies no bounds checks, no allow-list for field values (e.g. `op` could be an arbitrary string), and no maximum length limits on `name` or `note`. Fields like `scenario.name` are embedded in log messages and eventually in the lock file. While none of these paths currently reach a shell interpolation, adding manifest schema validation (e.g. Zod or a hand-written validator with strict allow-lists for `op`, `name`, `files`) would harden the build pipeline against malicious or malformed corpus manifests from compromised upstream sources.

title: WASM module loaded from a URL has no SRI integrity verification before compilation
description: The `loadBrowserModule` function (line 56) fetches the WASM binary from `wasmUrl` and compiles it directly without verifying its cryptographic integrity. The build manifest (`build-manifest.json`) records `wasmIntegrity` (sha384 SRI hash) per tier, but this hash is never checked by the loader. An MITM attacker or a CDN serving stale/tampered content could substitute a malicious WASM binary that would be compiled and executed in the user's browser. The fix would be to use the `integrity` option in the `fetch` call (Subresource Integrity via `fetch(url, {integrity: manifestEntry.wasmIntegrity})`) or verify the sha384 of the arrayBuffer before passing to `WebAssembly.compile`.
